title: Mobile System Design
description: Design patterns for real-world mobile architecture: offline-first, image loading, feeds, and more
lesson:
Mobile System Design Interviews

Unlike backend system design (distributed systems, databases), mobile system design focuses on:

  • Offline-first architecture — how does the app work without internet?
  • Data sync — how do you reconcile local and remote state?
  • Performance — smooth scrolling, fast startup, low memory
  • Architecture layers — UI → ViewModel → Repository → DataSource
  • Caching — memory cache → disk cache → network

System Design Interview Framework

Use this five-step framework for every mobile system design question. Interviewers expect structured thinking, not just code.

Step 1: REQUIREMENTS (5 min) ├── Functional: What does the user DO? ├── Non-functional: Offline? Real-time? Scale? └── Constraints: Platform? API budget? Battery? Step 2: ARCHITECTURE (10 min) ├── Draw the layer diagram (UI → Domain → Data) ├── Identify key components (ViewModel, Repository, DB, API) └── Data flow: who owns what state? Step 3: DATA MODEL & STORAGE (5 min) ├── Entity design (Room tables, API DTOs) ├── Single source of truth (local DB vs remote) └── Caching strategy (TTL, invalidation) Step 4: API DESIGN (5 min) ├── REST endpoints or WebSocket events ├── Pagination strategy (cursor vs offset) └── Request/response shapes Step 5: TRADE-OFFS & DEEP DIVES (10 min) ├── What breaks at scale? ├── Offline vs real-time consistency └── Battery, memory, bandwidth constraints
Pro tip: Spend the first 5 minutes asking clarifying questions. Interviewers deliberately leave requirements vague to see if you ask. "Should this work offline?" and "How many items in the list?" are the kind of questions that show senior thinking.

The Standard Mobile Architecture

Almost every well-designed Android app follows this layered architecture. It is the foundation for all system design answers.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ UI LAYER │ │ Compose screens, navigation, themes │ │ Observes StateFlow from ViewModel │ └──────────────────┬──────────────────────┘ │ StateFlow / UiState ┌──────────────────▼──────────────────────┐ │ VIEWMODEL LAYER │ │ Holds UI state, handles user actions │ │ Calls use cases or repository directly │ └──────────────────┬──────────────────────┘ │ suspend functions ┌──────────────────▼──────────────────────┐ │ DOMAIN LAYER (optional) │ │ Use cases / interactors │ │ Business logic independent of Android │ └──────────────────┬──────────────────────┘ │ ┌──────────────────▼──────────────────────┐ │ DATA LAYER │ │ Repository = single source of truth │ │ ┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ │ Local Source │ │ Remote Source│ │ │ │ (Room DB) │ │ (Retrofit) │ │ │ └─────────────┘ └──────────────┘ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
// Repository — coordinates local and remote sources class ArticleRepository( private val api: NewsApi, private val dao: ArticleDao ) { // Room Flow = single source of truth (auto-emits on DB change) fun getArticles(): Flow<List<Article>> = dao.getAllArticles() suspend fun refresh() { try { val fresh = api.getLatestArticles() dao.insertAll(fresh) // Room Flow auto-emits update } catch (e: IOException) { // Network error — cached data still available } } }

Design: Offline-First Chat App

A chat app is the canonical mobile system design question. It tests offline handling, real-time transport, sync, and optimistic updates.

┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ Compose UI │────▶│ ViewModel │────▶│ ChatRepo │ │ LazyColumn │◀────│ StateFlow │◀────│ │ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────┬───────┘ │ ┌───────────────────┼───────────────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌────────────┐ │ Room DB │ │ WebSocket │ │ WorkManager│ │ messages tbl │ │ real-time │ │ sync queue│ │ (source of │ │ send/recv │ │ offline │ │ truth) │ │ │ │ retry │ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └────────────┘

Key design decisions:

// Message entity with delivery status @Entity(tableName = "messages") data class Message( @PrimaryKey val id: String = UUID.randomUUID().toString(), val chatId: String, val text: String, val senderId: String, val timestamp: Long = System.currentTimeMillis(), val status: MessageStatus = MessageStatus.PENDING ) enum class MessageStatus { PENDING, // saved locally, not yet sent SENT, // server acknowledged receipt DELIVERED, // recipient device received READ, // recipient opened the chat FAILED // send failed after retries } // Optimistic send: show immediately, sync in background class ChatRepository( private val dao: MessageDao, private val webSocket: ChatWebSocket, private val workManager: WorkManager ) { fun getMessages(chatId: String): Flow<List<Message>> = dao.getMessages(chatId) // Room Flow, single source of truth suspend fun sendMessage(chatId: String, text: String) { // 1. Save locally with PENDING status (instant UI update) val msg = Message(chatId = chatId, text = text, senderId = currentUserId, status = MessageStatus.PENDING) dao.insert(msg) // 2. Try WebSocket if connected if (webSocket.isConnected) { try { webSocket.send(msg) dao.updateStatus(msg.id, MessageStatus.SENT) } catch (e: Exception) { // 3. Queue for WorkManager retry if WebSocket fails enqueueSync(msg.id) } } else { enqueueSync(msg.id) } } private fun enqueueSync(messageId: String) { val work = OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<SendMessageWorker>() .setConstraints(Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.CONNECTED).build()) .setBackoffCriteria(BackoffPolicy.EXPONENTIAL, 30, TimeUnit.SECONDS) .setInputData(workDataOf("messageId" to messageId)) .build() workManager.enqueue(work) } }

Design: Image Loading Library

Understanding how Coil/Glide work internally is essential. The three-level cache is the core concept.

Request: load("https://example.com/photo.jpg") │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────┐ │ 1. MEMORY CACHE │ Speed: ~0.1ms │ LruCache<String, Bitmap>│ Size: % of app heap (~32MB) │ Decoded bitmaps │ Eviction: LRU │ Key: URL + size + xform│ └─────────┬───────────────┘ │ MISS ▼ ┌─────────────────────────┐ │ 2. DISK CACHE │ Speed: ~5ms │ DiskLruCache │ Size: 250MB default │ Encoded files (JPEG) │ Eviction: LRU │ Key: URL hash │ └─────────┬───────────────┘ │ MISS ▼ ┌─────────────────────────┐ │ 3. NETWORK FETCH │ Speed: 100-2000ms │ OkHttp + HTTP cache │ Respects Cache-Control │ Download → decode → │ headers │ downsample → cache │ └─────────────────────────┘
// Simplified image loader architecture class ImageLoader( private val memoryCache: LruCache<String, Bitmap>, private val diskCache: DiskLruCache, private val httpClient: OkHttpClient ) { suspend fun load(url: String, width: Int, height: Int): Bitmap { val key = "${url}_${width}x${height}" // 1. Memory cache — instant memoryCache.get(key)?.let { return it } // 2. Disk cache — fast val diskBitmap = withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { diskCache.get(url)?.let { snapshot -> decodeSampled(snapshot.inputStream(), width, height) } } diskBitmap?.let { memoryCache.put(key, it) return it } // 3. Network — slow but definitive return withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { val bytes = httpClient.fetchBytes(url) diskCache.put(url, bytes) // cache encoded val bitmap = decodeSampled(bytes, width, height) memoryCache.put(key, bitmap) // cache decoded bitmap } } // Downsample to target size (saves 4-16x memory) private fun decodeSampled( data: InputStream, reqW: Int, reqH: Int ): Bitmap { // BitmapFactory.Options: inJustDecodeBounds=true first, // calculate inSampleSize, then decode at reduced size val options = BitmapFactory.Options().apply { inSampleSize = calculateInSampleSize(data, reqW, reqH) } return BitmapFactory.decodeStream(data, null, options)!! } }
Memory trap: A 4000x3000 photo at ARGB_8888 = 48MB in memory. Always downsample to the ImageView's actual dimensions. A 200x200 thumbnail = 0.16MB. That is a 300x reduction.

Design: Social Media Feed

Feed design tests pagination, caching, prefetching, smooth scrolling, and DiffUtil for efficient updates.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ COMPOSE UI │ │ LazyColumn + collectAsLazyPagingItems() │ │ ├── Pull-to-refresh (SwipeRefresh) │ │ ├── Item composables (image + text + actions)│ │ └── Loading footer (next page indicator) │ └───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┘ │ ┌───────────────────────▼───────────────────────┐ │ PAGING 3 LAYER │ │ Pager(config = PagingConfig(pageSize = 20)) │ │ RemoteMediator: fetches from API → Room │ │ PagingSource: reads from Room (offline-first)│ └───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┘ │ ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐ ▼ ▼ ┌──────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ Room DB │ │ REST API │ │ posts table │ │ GET /feed │ │ + remote_keys │ │ ?cursor=X │ │ (cursor tracking│ │ &limit=20 │ └──────────────────┘ └──────────────────┘
// RemoteMediator — bridges network and database for Paging 3 class FeedRemoteMediator( private val api: FeedApi, private val db: AppDatabase ) : RemoteMediator<Int, PostEntity>() { override suspend fun load( loadType: LoadType, state: PagingState<Int, PostEntity> ): MediatorResult { val cursor = when (loadType) { LoadType.REFRESH -> null LoadType.PREPEND -> return MediatorResult.Success( endOfPaginationReached = true ) LoadType.APPEND -> db.remoteKeyDao().getNextCursor() } return try { val response = api.getFeed(cursor = cursor, limit = 20) db.withTransaction { if (loadType == LoadType.REFRESH) { db.postDao().clearAll() } db.postDao().insertAll(response.posts) db.remoteKeyDao().saveCursor(response.nextCursor) } MediatorResult.Success( endOfPaginationReached = response.nextCursor == null ) } catch (e: IOException) { MediatorResult.Error(e) } } }

Performance optimizations for feeds:

  • Prefetching: Paging 3 prefetchDistance loads the next page before the user scrolls to it
  • DiffUtil / keys: items(key = { it.id }) in LazyColumn skips recomposition for unchanged items
  • Image preloading: Start loading images for items just off-screen using Coil's ImageLoader.enqueue()
  • Stable IDs: Use server-provided IDs as keys so items maintain scroll position across refreshes
  • Placeholder shimmer: Show content-shaped placeholders while loading to reduce perceived latency

Design: Real-Time Location Sharing

Location sharing combines foreground services, battery optimization, and batching.

// Foreground service for continuous location updates class LocationSharingService : Service() { private lateinit var fusedClient: FusedLocationProviderClient override fun onCreate() { super.onCreate() fusedClient = LocationServices.getFusedLocationProviderClient(this) } fun startTracking() { val request = LocationRequest.Builder( Priority.PRIORITY_HIGH_ACCURACY, 10_000L // 10 second interval ).apply { setMinUpdateIntervalMillis(5_000L) setMaxUpdateDelayMillis(30_000L) // batch up to 30s }.build() fusedClient.requestLocationUpdates(request, locationCallback, mainLooper) } // Batch locations and send periodically to save battery private val locationBuffer = mutableListOf<Location>() private val locationCallback = object : LocationCallback() { override fun onLocationResult(result: LocationResult) { locationBuffer.addAll(result.locations) if (locationBuffer.size >= 3) { // send every ~30s sendBatch(locationBuffer.toList()) locationBuffer.clear() } } } }
Battery considerations:
- Active sharing: Foreground service + notification (required by Android). High accuracy, 5-10s intervals.
- Background tracking: WorkManager periodic (15 min minimum). Significant location changes only.
- Geofencing: System handles monitoring, wakes your app on enter/exit. Most battery-efficient.

Network Resilience Patterns

Mobile networks are unreliable. Every production app needs these patterns.

1. Retry with Exponential Backoff

// Retry with exponential backoff + jitter suspend fun <T> retryWithBackoff( maxRetries: Int = 3, initialDelayMs: Long = 1000, maxDelayMs: Long = 30_000, block: suspend () -> T ): T { var currentDelay = initialDelayMs repeat(maxRetries - 1) { try { return block() } catch (e: IOException) { delay(currentDelay + Random.nextLong(0, currentDelay / 2)) // jitter currentDelay = (currentDelay * 2).coerceAtMost(maxDelayMs) } } return block() // last attempt — let it throw }

2. Circuit Breaker

// Circuit breaker — stop hitting a failing service class CircuitBreaker( private val failureThreshold: Int = 5, private val resetTimeMs: Long = 60_000 ) { private var failureCount = 0 private var lastFailureTime = 0L private var state = State.CLOSED // CLOSED = normal operation enum class State { CLOSED, OPEN, HALF_OPEN } suspend fun <T> execute(block: suspend () -> T): T { when (state) { State.OPEN -> { if (System.currentTimeMillis() - lastFailureTime > resetTimeMs) { state = State.HALF_OPEN // try one request } else { throw CircuitOpenException("Service unavailable") } } else -> { /* proceed */ } } return try { val result = block() onSuccess() result } catch (e: Exception) { onFailure() throw e } } private fun onSuccess() { failureCount = 0; state = State.CLOSED } private fun onFailure() { failureCount++ lastFailureTime = System.currentTimeMillis() if (failureCount >= failureThreshold) state = State.OPEN } }

3. Request Deduplication

// Deduplicate concurrent identical requests class RequestDeduplicator { private val inFlight = mutableMapOf<String, Deferred<Any>>() private val mutex = Mutex() suspend fun <T> deduplicate( key: String, block: suspend () -> T ): T { val existing = mutex.withLock { inFlight[key] } if (existing != null) { @Suppress("UNCHECKED_CAST") return existing.await() as T // reuse in-flight request } val deferred = coroutineScope { async { block() }.also { mutex.withLock { inFlight[key] = it as Deferred<Any> } } } return try { deferred.await() } finally { mutex.withLock { inFlight.remove(key) } } } } // Example: 10 composables request the same user profile → 1 network call

Caching Strategies Comparison

StrategyHow It WorksBest ForDownside
TTL (Time to Live)Cache expires after fixed duration (e.g., 5 min)Data that changes predictably (weather, stock prices)Can serve stale data; can refetch unnecessarily
ETag / If-None-MatchServer returns ETag hash. Client sends it on next request. Server returns 304 (not modified) or new data.Data that changes rarely (user profile, settings)Still requires a network round-trip every time
Stale-While-RevalidateReturn cached data immediately, fetch fresh data in background, update cache for next requestContent feeds, dashboards, any data where freshness is desired but not criticalUser sees stale data briefly on first load
Cache-then-NetworkEmit cached data first, then emit network data (two emissions from the same Flow)Offline-first apps, news readersUI updates twice — can cause layout shifts
Network-onlyAlways fetch from network, no local cacheReal-time data (live scores, stock trading)No offline support; slow on bad networks
Write-throughWrite to both cache and server synchronouslyCritical data (payment, auth tokens)Slower writes; fails if network is down
Write-behindWrite to cache immediately, sync to server later (WorkManager)Offline-capable writes (drafts, likes, messages)Conflict resolution needed on sync
Common interview mistake: Saying "just cache it" without specifying the invalidation strategy. Caching is easy; invalidation is the hard part. Always state when cached data expires and what happens when it's stale.

questions.title: Design an Offline-First News Reader
questions.description: Design the architecture for a news reader app that works offline. Describe the data flow, caching strategy, and sync mechanism.
questions.answer: Architecture: UI observes ViewModel StateFlow → ViewModel collects from Repository → Repository uses Room DB as single source of truth. On launch: (1) Immediately show cached articles from Room. (2) Fetch fresh articles from API in background. (3) Insert new articles into Room. (4) Room Flow automatically emits updated list to UI. Cache strategy: Room stores articles with timestamps. On fetch, compare timestamps and merge (insert new, update changed, optionally delete old). Stale data policy: show cached data immediately, show refresh indicator while fetching, show error only if cache is empty AND network fails. Pull-to-refresh triggers a forced network fetch. This is the "single source of truth" pattern — UI always reads from Room, never directly from network.
questions.explanation: The key insight is making the local database the single source of truth. The network is just a way to update the database. This gives you: instant startup (cached data), offline support (Room works without network), consistency (one source, no conflicts), and reactive updates (Room returns Flows that auto-emit on changes).
hints: Think: where does the UI read from? Where does the network write to?
The "single source of truth" pattern: DB is truth, network updates DB

questions.title: Repository with Cache Strategy
questions.description: Write a Repository that implements the offline-first pattern: return cached data immediately, fetch fresh data in background, update cache.
questions.explanation: The Repository exposes Room's Flow directly. When the ViewModel collects this Flow, it gets cached data immediately. refreshArticles() fetches from the API and writes to Room — the Flow automatically re-emits with updated data. The UI gets updates reactively without polling. This clean separation means the UI doesn't care WHERE the data came from.
hints: Room DAOs can return Flow<List<T>> — they auto-emit when the table changes
The network write goes INTO the database, the UI reads FROM the database

questions.title: Image Loading Architecture
questions.description: When designing an image loading library (like Coil/Glide), what is the correct cache hierarchy?
options: Memory cache (LRU HashMap) → Disk cache (file storage) → Network fetch — check fastest first
Load everything into memory on app start
questions.explanation: The three-level cache: Memory (LruCache, ~50ms, limited by heap) → Disk (DiskLruCache, ~5ms from SSD, large capacity) → Network (100-2000ms, unlimited). Always check the fastest cache first. Memory cache uses decoded Bitmaps for instant display. Disk cache stores encoded files (JPEG/PNG). Network is the fallback. Images are downsized to the ImageView dimensions before caching to save memory. Lifecycle-aware loading cancels requests when the view is detached.
hints: Which storage is fastest? Which is slowest?
Think about the tradeoff between speed and capacity at each level

questions.title: Design a Chat App Architecture
questions.description: Design the message delivery architecture for a mobile chat app. How do you handle real-time messages, offline sending, and delivery status?
questions.answer: Real-time: WebSocket connection for live messages. When connected, messages arrive instantly and are saved to Room DB. When disconnected, FCM push notifications trigger a sync. Offline sending: Messages typed offline go into a "pending" queue in Room with status PENDING. When connection restores, a SyncWorker (WorkManager) sends pending messages in order. Status flow: PENDING → SENT (server ACK) → DELIVERED (recipient received) → READ (recipient opened). UI shows status indicators. Optimistic UI: Show the message immediately in the chat with PENDING status — don't wait for server confirmation. If send fails after retries, mark as FAILED with a retry button. Architecture: ChatRepository holds WebSocket + Room. Messages always write to Room first (offline-first). WebSocket listener writes incoming messages to Room. Room Flow drives the UI.
questions.explanation: Key design decisions: (1) Offline-first: Room is truth, network syncs. (2) Optimistic updates: show immediately, update status later. (3) Message ordering: use server timestamps for ordering, local timestamps for display while pending. (4) Dual transport: WebSocket for real-time, FCM for wake-up when app is killed.
hints: How does WhatsApp show messages you typed while offline?
Think about the message lifecycle: pending → sent → delivered → read

questions.title: Pagination Pattern
questions.description: What is the recommended way to implement infinite scrolling pagination in a modern Android app?
options: Load all data at once and filter locally
Use Paging 3 library with PagingSource + LazyColumn + collectAsLazyPagingItems(). It handles loading states, caching, and scroll position automatically
Manually track page numbers and append to a list in the ViewModel
questions.explanation: Paging 3 provides: PagingSource (defines how to load pages from network/DB), Pager (configures page size and creates PagingData flow), collectAsLazyPagingItems() (Compose integration). It handles: automatic next-page loading, loading/error states per page, separator insertion, caching with RemoteMediator for offline-first, and efficient memory usage (drops pages not visible). It integrates with both Room and Retrofit.
hints: Google provides a library specifically for this — it handles the hard parts

questions.title: Optimistic Update Pattern
questions.description: Write a "like" function that shows the like immediately (optimistic) and reverts if the API call fails.
questions.explanation: The optimistic update pattern: (1) Save current state for rollback, (2) Update immediately for instant feedback, (3) Make API call in background, (4) Revert if it fails. This makes the app feel instant — the user sees the like immediately instead of waiting 200ms+ for a network round trip. Social media apps (Instagram, Twitter) all use this pattern.
hints: Save the old state BEFORE modifying, so you can restore it on error
Update the StateFlow immediately, then try the API call
